cataloging_subject.label: Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous, pro-Yorkist prose chronicle of Edward IV's return and restoration in 1471. It comprises three internally introduced narrative parts and claims compilation by a servant of Edward IV from personal observation and participant reports. It is not the later title page, Harley MS 543 as a whole, the lost Fleetwood exemplar as a physical object, John Bruce's complete 1838 Camden Society volume, the University of Michigan encoding, the Internet Archive scan, or the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous (described as a servant of Edward IV)
catalog_summary.region: England (Yorkist royal milieu; exact place unknown)

titles.value: Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI

assertion.notes: An established access title, normalized only by omitting the date suffix and final punctuation; the surviving title page in Harley MS 543 is cataloged as later or in another hand rather than authorial.

titles.value: The Historie of the Arrivall of King Edward IV
The Historie of the arrivall of K. Edward IV. in England and the finall recoverie of his kingdomes from Henry VI. in Anno Domini 1471

assertion.notes: Current BL catalog transcription of the title attached to the Harley witness; Bruce says its title page is in another hand.

titles.value: In the yere of grace 1471, aftar the comptinge of the churche of England

agents.name: Anonymous servant of Edward IV
notes: The opening argument claims that a servant of the king compiled the narrative after seeing much of the action and learning the rest from participants. No inspected source supplies a personal name; eyewitness status is an internal claim, not independently verified identity.

assertion.notes: The social role and eyewitness basis are asserted by the text. The author remains unnamed, and Whittle treats more specific institutional or shared-authorship proposals as hypotheses.
Stow transcribed the lost exemplar formerly in William Fleetwood's possession; his late-sixteenth-century copy survives in Harley MS 543, ff. 31r-49r.

scope.description: The 1838 Camden Society transcription, title, punctuation, supplied readings, and notes

date_statements.display: 1471, after 26 May; probably composed soon after the narrated campaign

assertion.notes: The narrative reaches 26 May 1471 and presents itself as a contemporary servant's account. The exact completion date and place are unstated.

The surviving Stow transcript is dated by the current BL catalog to the fourth quarter of the sixteenth century. The 1471 composition and lost Fleetwood exemplar are distinct from this first surviving attestation; reports of earlier circulation do not supply an extant earlier copy.
Whittle identifies Bruce's Camden Society edition as the first publication of the complete long English text; earlier derivative use in Holinshed is not treated as publication of this version.
The account is written from Edward IV's English royal/Yorkist perspective. No inspected source fixes its place of composition more precisely.
The surviving long version is late Middle English. Modern English title-page and editorial matter in Bruce's edition are representation layers, not additional work-level languages.

form_statements.description: Continuous prose chronicle divided into three internally introduced narrative parts.

subject_statements.label: Wars of the Roses and political news

assertion.notes: The narrative's partisan framing is explicit; propaganda is a modern analytical classification supported by Whittle.

summaries.value: A strongly pro-Yorkist account of Edward IV's departure from Zeeland, return to England, recovery of support, victories at Barnet and Tewkesbury, defeat of Thomas Neville's assault on London, and final settlement in May 1471. Its opening claims compilation by an unnamed royal servant who witnessed much of the campaign and obtained the remainder from participants.

CME00129 encodes Bruce's edition title page and the complete chronicle on numbered pp. 1-40. It omits Bruce's introduction, the blank leaf before the chronicle, notes on pp. 41-47, the index, and all other volume matter. Within the chronicle it preserves all three narrative parts, their arguments, and the final Fleetwood-book trailer.

The XML contains two DIV1, three DIV2, two HEAD, three ARGUMENT, 51 P, 41 PB, three GAP, three HI, 21 body SUP, and one TRAILER elements. Twenty-six literal square-bracketed supplies occur in the encoded chronicle. The three GAP elements represent one-word blanks printed by Bruce, not demonstrated physical damage. The encoded title page says PUBLISHED FOR where the inspected 1838 page says PRINTED FOR.

extent.description: One prose chronicle in three internally introduced narrative parts, occupying Bruce's numbered pp. 1-40; the separate encoded edition title page is editorial paratext and is not counted as a work part.

content_parts.label: Edward IV's return, advance, and victory at Barnet
content_parts.summary: From Edward's departure from Zeeland and landing at Ravenspur through the northern advance, return to London, and defeat of Warwick at Barnet.

locators.value: within ff. 31r-49r; exact internal folio boundary not publicly verified

content_parts.label: Queen Margaret's campaign and the battle of Tewkesbury
content_parts.summary: Queen Margaret and Prince Edward gather forces in the west; Edward IV pursues and defeats them at Tewkesbury.

locators.value: within ff. 31r-49r; exact internal folio boundary not publicly verified

content_parts.label: Fauconberg's assault on London and final settlement
content_parts.summary: Thomas Neville, the Bastard of Fauconberg, attacks London; the assault fails, Edward returns, and Fauconberg surrenders his ships at Sandwich on 26 May.

locators.value: within ff. 31r-49r; exact internal folio boundary not publicly verified

evidence.citation: CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00129.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for artifact identity, encoded title page, text, internal arguments, gaps, supplies, page boundaries, trailer, Middle English language declaration, and 2016 revision history.

evidence.citation: Local structural and textual audit of CME00129.xml against Bruce's 1838 page images.
evidence.summary: Counted structural elements and supplies, mapped the complete chronicle to printed pp. 1-40, verified the three printed blanks, and identified the encoded PUBLISHED/printed PRINTED title-page discrepancy.

evidence.citation: John Bruce, ed., Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV. in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI. A.D. M.CCCC.LXXI. (London: Printed for the Camden Society by John Bowyer Nichols and Son, 1838), title page; introduction pp. xiii-xiv; chronicle pp. 1-40; notes pp. 41-47.
evidence.locator: Title page physical PDF p. 5/BookReader n5; introduction physical pp. 17-18; chronicle printed pp. 1-40 = physical PDF pp. 21-60 = BookReader n21-n60
evidence.summary: Complete source edition, visually inspected for publication data, exact encoded boundary, work title and contents, anonymous-servant claim, printed supplies and blanks, Stow/Fleetwood lineage, and excluded introduction, notes, and index.

evidence.citation: British Library, Harley MS 543, 'Collection of English historical texts,' record 040-002046372.
evidence.locator: Scope and Content ff. 31r-49r; Physical Characteristics; Date Range; Custodial History; Digitised Content
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the exact target folios and trailer, Stow's hand, late-sixteenth-century date, paper support, whole-codex extent and dimensions, provenance, access route, and blank digitised-content field.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, 'Historie of the arrivall of Edward IV. in England,' CME00129.
evidence.summary: Official item page confirming title, John Bruce as edition creator, London 1838 source citation, public electronic text, contents structure, and public-domain/No Copyright - United States rights statement.

evidence.citation: Andrew R. Whittle, The Historical Reputation of Edward IV 1461-1725 (PhD thesis, University of East Anglia, 2017), pp. 51-61 and 159.
evidence.locator: Printed pp. 51-61 = physical PDF pp. 61-71; printed p. 159 = physical PDF pp. 169-170
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly discussion of the long Arrivall's Yorkist purpose, anonymous royal-servant claim, survival in Stow's Harley 543 transcript from Fleetwood's lost book, Bruce's first complete publication, and disputed relationships to shorter French and English texts.
notes: The complete 359-page PDF renders and yields text. qpdf reports nonfatal linearization hint-table mismatches; it is not described as qpdf-clean.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous servant of Edward IV be connected securely to the privy seal or to the author of the Lincolnshire Chronicle?
next_steps: Review the specialist studies cited by Whittle and later scholarship.
Retain anonymous authorship and treat institutional or shared-authorship proposals as hypotheses until direct evidence is established.

open_questions.question: What is the direction and sequence among the long English Arrivall, shorter English text, and French newsletter versions?
next_steps: Consult modern textual studies and editions of all versions.
Do not place a French short text upstream of this work solely on Bruce's nineteenth-century abridgment claim.

open_questions.question: Why does CME00129 transcribe the 1838 title page as PUBLISHED FOR when the inspected page reads PRINTED FOR?
next_steps: Check whether U-M encoded a different physical copy or whether this is a transcription error.
Correct the XML only in a separately scoped encoding change after verification.

notes: William Fleetwood is the recorded former owner of the lost exemplar and the source of a downstream adapted narrative in Holinshed, not the author of the Arrivall under the inspected evidence.
The Fleetwood exemplar is not labeled an authorial autograph; its exact date and physical history are unresolved.
The three GAP elements encode blanks visible in Bruce's print and are not evidence of lacunae caused by present damage to Harley MS 543.
